mcqs.question: Which disk scheduling algorithm is also known as the elevator algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: SCAN algorithm is called the elevator algorithm because it moves the head in one direction servicing all requests, then reverses direction like an elevator.

mcqs.question: Which algorithm services the request closest to the current head position?
mcqs.explanation: SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) always selects the request that is closest to the current head position to minimize seek time.

mcqs.question: What is the major disadvantage of SSTF?
mcqs.explanation: SSTF can cause starvation where requests far from the current head position may never be serviced if new requests keep appearing closer to the head.

mcqs.question: In C-SCAN, what happens after the head reaches the end of the disk?

options.text: It returns to the beginning without servicing requests
It services remaining requests in reverse order

mcqs.explanation: C-SCAN returns to the beginning of the disk without servicing any requests on the return trip, providing more uniform wait times.

mcqs.question: Which component contributes the most to disk access time?
mcqs.explanation: Seek time, the time taken for the head to move to the correct track, is typically the largest component of disk access time.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of FCFS disk scheduling?
mcqs.explanation: FCFS processes each request exactly once in order of arrival, giving O(n) time complexity.

mcqs.question: Which algorithm provides the most uniform wait times?
mcqs.explanation: C-SCAN provides more uniform wait times because all requests are serviced in one pass and the return trip doesn't service any requests.

mcqs.question: LOOK algorithm differs from SCAN in that:

options.text: LOOK doesn't go to the end of the disk

mcqs.explanation: LOOK only moves the head as far as the last request in each direction, whereas SCAN goes to the end of the disk regardless of requests.

mcqs.question: Which disk scheduling algorithm is best for systems with light load?
mcqs.explanation: Under light load, FCFS works well because there are few requests, so seek time is naturally minimal and FCFS has no overhead.

mcqs.question: In a disk with 200 tracks (0-199), if the head is at track 50 and the request queue is 82, 170, 43, 94, 25, 110, using SSTF, which is serviced first?
mcqs.explanation: From track 50, the closest request is track 43 (distance 7), compared to 25 (distance 25), 82 (distance 32), 94 (distance 44), 170 (distance 120).

mcqs.question: What is rotational latency?

options.text: Time to move head to correct track
Time for sector to rotate under head
Time for controller to process request

mcqs.explanation: Rotational latency is the time waiting for the desired sector to rotate to the read/write head position after the head reaches the correct track.

mcqs.question: C-LOOK stands for:
mcqs.explanation: C-LOOK stands for Circular LOOK, which is a variant of LOOK that circulates back to the first request rather than going to the disk ends.

mcqs.question: Which algorithm may cause请求饥饿 in SSTF?
mcqs.explanation: In SSTF, if requests keep arriving near the current head position, distant requests may never be serviced (starvation).

mcqs.question: The total head movement for FCFS with requests at tracks 95, 180, 34, 119, 12, 190 starting from track 50 is:
Which disk scheduling algorithm is considered fair?
mcqs.explanation: FCFS is considered the most fair algorithm because it services requests in exact order of arrival without prioritizing any request.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of SCAN over SSTF?
mcqs.explanation: SCAN eliminates starvation because it services all requests in its path, unlike SSTF which may indefinitely delay distant requests.

mcqs.question: In C-SCAN, the head returns to the beginning without servicing requests to ensure:
mcqs.explanation: C-SCAN returns without servicing to provide more predictable and uniform wait times for all requests.

mcqs.question: Disk access time formula includes:

options.text: Seek time + Rotational latency + Transfer time

mcqs.explanation: Disk access time = Seek time + Rotational latency + Data transfer time. All three components contribute to total access time.

mcqs.question: For a heavily loaded system, which algorithm typically performs best?
mcqs.explanation: For heavily loaded systems, SCAN or LOOK typically perform best because they minimize seek time while avoiding starvation.

mcqs.question: The 'arm' in disk scheduling refers to:
mcqs.explanation: The arm refers to the assembly that holds the read/write heads, which moves across the disk surface to access different tracks.
